draft.content_markdown:
# [anti_pattern] Prune duplicate remedies after no-gain failure cycles
## Signal
Repeated attempts to fix the same class of problem create multiple overlapping remedies, but measured outcomes do not improve.

## Practice
Do not keep adding narrowly scoped, failure-specific fixes when they duplicate an existing capability and produce no measurable gain. Instead, pause after a no-improvement cycle, compare the remedies for overlap, merge them into one clear general procedure, and retire redundant variants to reduce context clutter and maintenance cost. What not to repeat: accumulating near-identical “special case” fixes just because earlier attempts failed.

## Contraindications
Do not apply this pruning step when the variants address genuinely different operating conditions, have distinct evidence of effectiveness, or must remain separate for audit, safety, or compatibility reasons.

## Evidence
Derived from source kind: evo_failure; source id: not provided in the record.

draft.content_markdown:
# [anti_pattern] Stop accumulating dead skills after repeated fix attempts
## Signal
Repeated failed attempts to resolve a problem cause the system to retain redundant helper skills that add no functional value and inflate context costs.
## Practice
When an iterative fix effort repeatedly fails, do not keep stacking additional resolution patterns or specialised skills in the agent's toolkit — they become dead weight that increases token consumption without improving outcomes. Instead, after one or two failed attempts, step back and reassess whether the approach itself is flawed rather than adding more procedural variants. If a skill set is not demonstrably improving task success rates, discard it before it accumulates.
## Contraindications
Do not discard accumulated skills prematurely if each variant addresses a distinct failure mode in a genuinely different failure context; the anti-pattern only applies when skills are redundant duplicates of the same failed strategy.
## Evidence
Source: evo_failure — evolution iteration where redundant circular_import and import_error resolution skills were retained after failed handle_task.py fixes, resulting in zero improvement (delta=+0.0000) and subsequent revert.

draft.topic: [anti_pattern] Generic error handling masks domain-specific failure modes, wasting resources
draft.rationale: Transfer-memory insight from evo_failure event evo_failure_160f857ce03d0807 (sanitiser_max=global_meta, abstraction=0.91).
draft.content_markdown:
# [anti_pattern] Generic error handling masks domain-specific failure modes, wasting resources
## Signal
A system repeatedly encounters a specific external error (insufficient credits) but classifies it as a generic runtime fault, triggering self-healing loops that consume resources without resolving the root cause.
## Practice
When a recurring error is tied to an external constraint or state (rate limits, billing, quota exhaustion, authentication expiry), identify it distinctly from generic failures and route it to an explicit handling path—such as a dedicated interceptor, fallback, or user alert—rather than applying generic retry or recovery logic. This prevents token waste from futile self-healing and surfaces the real issue for resolution.
## Contraindications
Do not apply when the error's domain-specific nature is unclear or when the system cannot reliably distinguish it from transient noise; premature specialisation can lead to brittle handling logic that misses genuine variability.
## Evidence
Source: evo_failure – evolution iteration where a 402 "Insufficient credits" error was treated as a generic RuntimeError, causing repeated self-healing loops with no improvement (delta=+0.0000) and subsequent reversion.
